title: Broken Horizontal Access Control on `/<username>/drafts`
description: The application exposes user draft listings under the username-scoped route `/<username>/drafts`, but access control is not enforced per user. Any authenticated user can request another user’s draft listing, including the `admin` account’s listing, and receive a normal `200 OK` response with that user’s drafts. This is a horizontal authorization failure because path-based user scoping is treated as sufficient authorization. In practice, any logged-in user can browse other users’ draft listings and discover draft identifiers and edit links that should be private.
steps:
1. Log in as a low-privilege user, e.g. `reconukdhlfv / ReconPass123!`.
2. Request your own drafts page: `GET /reconukdhlfv/drafts` and observe `200 OK`.
3. While still authenticated as the same user, request another user’s drafts page: `GET /reconbfsgbrl/drafts` and observe `200 OK` with that user’s drafts.
4. Request the admin drafts page: `GET /admin/drafts` and observe `200 OK` and admin draft content/listing.
5. Confirm the response contains draft actions/links such as `View and Edit`, proving unauthorized access to another user’s resources.

title: Broken Object-Level Authorization (BOLA/IDOR) on `/edit_draft/<id>` Enabling Unauthorized Read and Write
description: The draft edit endpoint `/edit_draft/<id>` does not enforce ownership checks on the referenced draft object. Any authenticated user who knows or can discover a draft ID can read another user’s draft contents and modify it. Unauthorized read was confirmed against both another low-privilege user’s draft and an admin-owned draft. Unauthorized write was confirmed using a reversible proof string against a controlled low-privilege user draft, then rolled back successfully. This issue allows cross-user tampering of draft content and breaks object ownership boundaries.
steps:
1. Log in as user A, e.g. `reconukdhlfv / ReconPass123!`.
2. Identify another user’s draft ID, e.g. draft ID `4` for `reconbfsgbrl`.
3. As user A, request `GET /edit_draft/4` and observe `200 OK` with prefilled fields belonging to user B.
4. Request another known foreign draft such as admin draft ID `1`: `GET /edit_draft/1` and observe admin draft data.
5. As user A, submit `POST /edit_draft/4` with body `title=B benign draft | PT-PROOF-F3UMP3` and `content=B benign content`.
6. Observe `302 Found` and redirection.
7. Log in as user B and request `GET /edit_draft/4` to confirm the unauthorized title change.
8. Roll back by posting the original values back to `/edit_draft/4` and verify restoration.

title: Username Enumeration via Differential Responses on `/<username>/drafts`
description: The route `/<username>/drafts` leaks whether a username exists. Existing usernames return a normal authenticated drafts page with `200 OK`, while nonexistent usernames return `404 Not Found` with the default framework error page. This allows attackers to enumerate valid usernames such as `admin` and registered users, which can assist password attacks, targeted phishing, or chaining with authorization weaknesses.
steps:
1. Authenticate as any low-privilege user.
2. Request a drafts page for a known real username, for example `GET /admin/drafts`, and observe `200 OK`.
3. Request a drafts page for a random nonexistent username, for example `GET /nosuchuserxyz/drafts`, and observe `404 Not Found`.
4. Compare the responses and confirm the difference reliably discloses whether a username is valid.

title: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) on `POST /create_draft`
description: The draft creation workflow accepts authenticated state-changing POST requests without anti-CSRF protection. The `/create_draft` form contains no CSRF token or equivalent hidden anti-forgery mechanism, and the endpoint accepted a forged-style request with attacker-controlled `Origin` and `Referer` values. This means that if an authenticated victim visits an attacker-controlled page, the attacker can cause the victim’s browser to create drafts on their behalf.
steps:
1. Log in as a victim user in a browser session.
2. Request `GET /create_draft` and observe the form contains `title` and `content` fields but no hidden CSRF token.
3. Reproduce a forged request using the victim’s authenticated session cookie: `POST /create_draft` with headers `Origin: http://attacker.test` and `Referer: http://attacker.test/csrf_create.html`, and body `title=CSRF-CREATE-PROOF-9d1e` and `content=csrf create benign proof`.
4. Observe `302 Found` and redirect.
5. Request `GET /reconukdhlfv/drafts` and confirm the new draft appears.

title: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) on `POST /edit_draft/<id>`
description: The draft editing workflow accepts authenticated state-changing POST requests without anti-CSRF protection. The edit form contains no anti-CSRF token, and the endpoint accepted a forged-style request with attacker-controlled `Origin` and `Referer` values. This means that if an authenticated victim visits an attacker-controlled page, the attacker can cause the victim’s browser to modify a draft without the victim’s intent.
steps:
1. Log in as a victim user who owns a draft, for example draft ID `4`.
2. Request `GET /edit_draft/4` and observe the form contains `title` and `content` fields but no hidden CSRF token.
3. Submit a forged-style request using the victim’s authenticated session cookie: `POST /edit_draft/4` with headers `Origin: http://attacker.test` and `Referer: http://attacker.test/csrf_edit.html`, and body `title=B benign draft | CSRF-PROOF-9d1e` and `content=B benign content`.
4. Observe `302 Found` and redirect.
5. Request `GET /edit_draft/4` and confirm the title has changed.
6. Roll back using the original values and verify restoration.

title: Insecure Session Cookie Attributes (`Secure` and `SameSite` Absent)
description: The application sets its session cookie without the `Secure` or `SameSite` attributes. Observed login response set `session=...; HttpOnly; Path=/`, and the logout clearing cookie also lacked `Secure` and `SameSite`. Without `Secure`, the cookie is not explicitly restricted to HTTPS transport. Without `SameSite`, browser-level CSRF resistance is weakened, which directly supports the confirmed CSRF issues on draft creation and editing.
steps:
1. Submit a valid login request: `POST /login`.
2. Observe the response header `Set-Cookie: session=...; HttpOnly; Path=/`.
3. Confirm that the cookie lacks `Secure` and `SameSite`.
4. Request `GET /logout`.
5. Observe the clearing cookie header and confirm `Secure` and `SameSite` are absent there as well.

title: Logout Does Not Invalidate the Previously Issued Session Token Server-Side
description: The logout function clears the browser cookie but does not invalidate the previously issued session token on the server. A previously captured session cookie remained valid after logout and could still be used to access authenticated functionality. If an attacker has copied or stolen a valid session token before logout, the token remains usable after the victim logs out.
steps:
1. Log in as a valid user and record the issued session cookie from the login response.
2. Call logout: `GET /logout`.
3. Observe the logout response clearing the browser cookie.
4. Manually replay the previously recorded authenticated cookie in a new request: `GET /dashboard` with header `Cookie: session=<pre-logout-cookie>`.
5. Observe that the server still accepts the old token and redirects to the authenticated drafts page.
6. Confirm that logout did not invalidate the token server-side.

title: Framework and Server Version Disclosure via Headers and Default Error Pages
description: The application discloses its framework and runtime details in the `Server` header and through default framework error page behavior. Observed header: `Server: Werkzeug/3.0.6 Python/3.8.17`. Default 404 pages also used Werkzeug-style content such as `The requested URL was not found on the server.` This improves attacker fingerprinting, narrows exploit research, and aids version-specific attack planning.
steps:
1. Request any normal page, for example `GET /login`.
2. Observe the response header `Server: Werkzeug/3.0.6 Python/3.8.17`.
3. Request a nonexistent route, for example `GET /nope`.
4. Observe the same `Server` header and a default `404 Not Found` page containing framework-identifying text.
5. Confirm that framework/runtime details are consistently exposed across responses.

title: Missing Security Headers (`Content-Security-Policy`, `X-Frame-Options`, `X-Content-Type-Options`, `Strict-Transport-Security`)
description: Multiple application responses were missing common security hardening headers: `Content-Security-Policy`, `X-Frame-Options`, `X-Content-Type-Options`, and `Strict-Transport-Security`. Their absence reduces defense-in-depth against script/content injection, clickjacking, MIME sniffing, and HTTPS enforcement on future visits.
steps:
1. Request representative pages such as `GET /`, `GET /login`, `GET /create_draft`, and `GET /edit_draft/4`.
2. Review the response headers.
3. Confirm that `Content-Security-Policy`, `X-Frame-Options`, `X-Content-Type-Options`, and `Strict-Transport-Security` are absent.
4. Repeat on a default error page such as `GET /nope` to confirm the issue is consistent across responses.
